message: 220 human-human dialogs.

Intent is annotated with a hierarchical scheme that first separates on-task and off-task intents. On-task intents are defined specifically for each task, while off-task intents use universal dialog-act categories consisting of six general intents and six additional social intents; annotation is done at the sentence level after segmenting each conversation turn into single sentences.
TransferTransfo and the hybrid dialog system combining vanilla TransferTransfo and MISSA.

The model is evaluated with automatic metrics: perplexity (PPL), response-intent prediction (RIP), response-slot prediction (RSP), extended response-intent prediction (ERIP), and extended response-slot prediction (ERSP); RIP/RSP compare predicted response intents/slots against human-annotated ground truth, while ERIP/ERSP allow partial credit based on acceptable intent/slot transitions estimated from training-data transition probabilities. Human evaluation uses five criteria: fluency, coherence, engagement, dialog length, and task success score, where task success is the average types of attacker information obtained (name, address, phone number).

OpenTapioca is not state-of-the-art overall: on ISTEX-1000 it achieves the best reported performance with 0.870 micro / 0.858 macro F1, but on RSS-500 it scores 0.335 / 0.310 versus AIDA’s 0.455 / 0.447, and on AIDA-CoNLL it scores 0.482 / 0.399 versus AIDA’s 0.725 / 0.684. On Microposts 2016, OpenTapioca has the best micro F1 (0.087) but not the best macro F1 (0.515 vs FREME NER’s 0.790).

Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012). The paper says both are state-of-the-art early rumour detection baselines that use various message-, user-, topic- and propagation-based features with an SVM classifier.

They report accuracy and also use the standard TDT evaluation procedure with official TDT3 scripts, which evaluates detection using Detection Error Trade-off (DET) curves showing miss vs. false alarm probability. They also measure efficiency by throughput per second.
No. They frame rumour detection as a classification problem, and the weight vector and detection threshold are learned to maximize prediction accuracy.
Yes. They state that, because no official redistributable datasets were available, they created their own dataset, including 202 confirmed rumours and 202 non-rumours.
Chinese.

They frame rumor detection as “a classification problem that assesses a document’s likelihood of becoming a future rumour at the time of its publication.” They also consider “unconfirmed (novel) information with respect to trusted resources” as an indication of rumours.
LDA, Doc-NADE, HTMM, and GMNTM.
The paper explores a generative document evaluation task, a document classification task, and a topic2sentence task.
CoNLL2003, OntoNotes 5.0, Chinese OntoNotes 4.0, MSRA, Weibo NER, and Resume NER.

They use relative positional encodings \(R_{t-j}\) based on the offset \(t-j\): \(R_{t-j}=[\ldots,\sin((t-j)/10000^{2i/d_k}),\cos((t-j)/10000^{2i/d_k}),\ldots]^T\), and compute attention scores as \(A^{rel}_{t,j}=Q_tK_j^T+Q_tR_{t-j}^T+uK_j^T+vR_{t-j}^T\). Because \(R_t\) and \(R_{-t}\) have opposite signs in the sine terms but the same cosine terms, the attention can distinguish both direction and relative distance.

Yes. The paper states that TENER achieves superior performance than prevailing BiLSTM-based models and unveils new state-of-the-art performance on two English NER datasets under the same embedding constraints, while the conclusion says it outperforms previous models on all six datasets under the same pre-trained embeddings and external knowledge.

The state-of-the-art systems mentioned for knowledge authoring evaluation are SEMAFOR, SLING, and Stanford KBP. For question answering on the MetaQA dataset, the paper refers only to a state-of-the-art machine learning approach [20] without naming it.

The question answering system was evaluated on two datasets: manually constructed general questions based on the 50 logical frames, and the MetaQA dataset. MetaQA contains almost 29,000 test questions and over 260,000 training questions.

The paper’s contributions are: adapting a state-of-the-art seq2seq neural system to Italian event detection and classification in a single-step approach; investigating the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task; and comparing against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier. It also makes the pre-trained models and scripts publicly available.

The paper uses as baseline the best system that participated in EVENTI Subtask B, FBK-HLT. FBK-HLT is a cascade of two SVM classifiers, one for detection and one for classification, based on rich linguistic features.
Yes. The conclusion notes that linguistically enriched embeddings “have proven to be very powerful for the same task in English,” indicating the approach can extend beyond Italian.

They assign each cluster word to a WordNet synset by choosing the set of synsets whose total pairwise distance is minimized, generate candidate labels as the union of all hypernyms of those chosen synsets, and then rank candidates by the sum of their distances to the chosen synsets using WordNet pathwise distance. The semantic concept label is the candidate label whose synset is closest overall to the cluster’s chosen synsets.

They train domain-specific Word2Vec CBOW embeddings for gender-associated words and then apply k-means clustering to those embeddings, running k-means 50 times and keeping the solution with the lowest sum of squared errors. They then label clusters by disambiguating each word to WordNet synsets that minimize within-cluster distance, generating candidate labels from the union of the synsets’ hypernyms, and ranking those labels by their total WordNet pathwise distance to the cluster synsets.
The two introduced datasets contain 15,917 texts in the celebrity dataset and 283,973 texts in the professor dataset. They comprise 342,645 and 976,677 sentences, respectively.

The strong baselines were the random baseline of 20% for the Word Intrusion task and the cluster centroid used as a baseline label for the cluster labeling task. The centroid achieved an in-cluster rate of .60 and an out-of-cluster rate of .18.

They usually respond poorly, lagging behind rule-based or commercial systems in perceived appropriateness. In particular, data-driven systems often produce flirtatious responses, and some also produce retaliatory responses.
They collected 9960 HITs from 472 crowd workers. After filtering, 18,826 scores remained.
No. The document evaluates annotation quality via inter-annotator agreement and evaluates NER models on the dataset, but it does not mention an evaluation of automatic annotation.

Agglutinative and fusional morphological typologies are considered. The paper also evaluates languages with varying degrees of morphological richness and typology: Turkish and Finnish as agglutinative, and Czech, German, Spanish, Catalan, and English as fusional.
Unanswerable
Gold morphological features: the lemma of the token followed by language-specific morphological tags. For some languages, additional information is included, such as part-of-speech tags for Turkish.

The paper uses the Semantic Scholar corpus (SemScholar), Springer Nature’s SciGraph, the Textbook Question Answering (TQA) corpus, the January 2018 English Wikipedia dataset, and the Flickr30K and COCO benchmarks.
Unanswerable
Unanswerable

The two supervised baselines were the direct combination baseline and the supervised pre-training baseline. They also report Acc vgg, where the visual branch is replaced with pre-trained VGG16 features learned on ImageNet.
Yes. The main data used is from scientific publications and science curricula, so it is specific to the scientific domain, although Wikipedia is noted as a general-purpose source.

From scientific literature, specifically a corpus of scientific figures and captions extracted from Springer Nature SciGraph and AI2 Semantic Scholar. In their dataset construction, they downloaded PDFs from Semantic Scholar and extracted figures and captions with PDFFigures2, and used the intersection with SciGraph because SciGraph itself does not provide PDF links.

No. The paper states that top-performing approaches could potentially benefit from lexicon-derived features, but this system “do[es] not experiment adding any additional sources of information as input” and works “without leveraging on lexicons.”

No. They explicitly state that although their model could include features such as lexicons, they “do not experiment adding any additional sources of information as input,” and only suggest lexicon-derived features for future work.
The baseline was the Weka baseline.

Their model outperformed the Weka baseline on the development set but performed worse on the test set, with average Pearson correlations of 0.689 vs. 0.611 on development and 0.522 vs. 0.648 on test. The best per-dataset test correlations for their model were 0.470 (Anger), 0.537 (Joy), 0.561 (Fear), and 0.520 (Sadness).

The dataset used was the training, validation, and test datasets provided for the EmoInt-2017 shared task, consisting of tweets annotated for four emotions: joy, sadness, fear, and anger. These tweets were annotated using Best-Worst Scaling (BWS).
Hate speech is defined here as speech that promotes the dehumanization of people who are typically from minority groups and can incite hate crime.
English, French, and Arabic.

Whether the text is direct or indirect; whether it is offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal; the attribute on which it discriminates; the name of the targeted group; and how annotators feel about its content on a negative-to-neutral sentiment range.
The final dataset has 5,647 English tweets, 4,014 French tweets, and 3,353 Arabic tweets, plus a pilot corpus of 100 tweets per language.

Semantic Web technologies present opportunities in MT for semantic and syntactic disambiguation, improved named entity recognition and translation, handling non-standard speech via user-context and linked idioms, and translating knowledge bases for use in language models. They can exploit structured knowledge, semantic annotations, reasoning, SPARQL queries, multilingual resources, and knowledge graph embeddings to improve translation quality, especially for ambiguous terms, entities, idioms, and low-resource scenarios.

The challenges associated with using Semantic Web technologies in Machine Translation are that applying SW techniques for semantic disambiguation is tedious to implement, especially for common words rather than named entities, and may require repeated application to achieve successful translation. In addition, using ontology reasoning to address syntactic disambiguation suffers from poor run-time performance, which must be addressed before reasoners can be successfully integrated into MT systems.

Linguistic properties/features are another obstacle. The paper also lists broader open challenges: excessive focus on English/European languages and low-resource pairs, cross-domain translation limitations, translating social-network non-standard text, difficult translation among morphologically rich languages, and mismatch between speech-translation training data and real user speech.

The EEG features used were five statistical features: root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis, and power spectral entropy. These were extracted from 31 channels, giving 155 features total, and later reduced with KPCA and augmented with delta and delta-delta features.
Unanswerable

The dataset used was built by the authors as two simultaneous speech-EEG recording databases, and for data set A the spoken sentences were the first 30 sentences from the USC-TIMIT database. Database A used 10 subjects with 40 dB background noise, and database B used 8 subjects with 65 dB background music noise.

No. The paper describes LadaBERT as preserving the original knowledge in the pre-trained teacher and compares it against baselines, but Table 3 shows LadaBERT remains below BERT-Base accuracy on all reported tasks.

Yes. They compare single methods and combinations, reporting that weight pruning generally outperforms matrix factorization alone, and that the combination of matrix factorization and weight pruning is better than single solutions, while LadaBERT’s full combination with knowledge distillation performs best overall.
MNLI-m, MNLI-mm, SST-2, QQP, and QNLI.
News text, specifically news articles from the DailyMail website.

They use two large-scale datasets created from DailyMail news articles and highlights for training: a sentence extraction dataset labeled on 200K documents and a word extraction dataset containing 170K articles. For evaluation, they use the DUC-2002 single-document summarization task and 500 articles from the DailyMail test set.
Yes. They include a neural abstractive summarization baseline (NN-ABS) and compare against it in both ROUGE and human evaluation.

Hierarchical commonsense knowledge, specifically ontologies with transitive entailment relations such as IS-A relations. They also mention more general non-hierarchical commonsense knowledge mined from raw text.

They mean embedding spaces whose geometry directly encodes structural constraints, so predictions automatically obey consistency properties like transitivity in ontologies. In the order-embedding model, this intrinsic geometry is a coordinate-wise partial order: a supercategory’s embedding is smaller than a subcategory’s embedding in every dimension.

Yes. They used pre-trained sentiment models, including a BiLSTM trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank and a character-level multiplicative LSTM trained on 82 million Amazon reviews, and these larger-corpus models were applied without further adaptation.

The models extracted n-grams, distributed semantic features from averaged pretrained word embeddings, and sentiment features from both manually constructed lexica and pre-trained sentiment embeddings. Specifically, the lexicon-based sentiment features were word percentages across 192 Empath lexical categories, including emotion classes such as surprise and disgust.
Unanswerable
text, graphics, arrows, and arrowheads.
No. The AI2D annotations were created by crowd-sourced non-expert annotators on Amazon Mechanical Turk, while AI2D-RST was annotated by trained experts.

They are compared by evaluating the two resources on the same diagrams as graph-based representations learned with graph neural networks in two experiments. The comparison focuses on how effectively crowd-sourced AI2D graphs and expert-annotated AI2D-RST graphs represent multimodal diagram structure, particularly for learning diagram element identity from layout features and diagram types.
Amazon Mechanical Turk.
The trained experts are the annotators who annotated the 1000-diagram AI2D-RST subset using a new multi-layer annotation schema for describing the diagrams in AI2D.
They opt for neural architectures using both a Hierarchical Attention Network (HAN) with bi-directional GRUs and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).

They use the Clueweb09-derived dataset of [4] for baseline comparison, and for cross-temporal, cross-topic, and cross-domain training/evaluation they generate a new dataset based on Wikipedia crawl data. This new dataset is built from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” pages from 2018 and 2009, plus linked pages and random negative seed pages.

Cross-domain appears to be the hardest setup. The reported F1 scores are lower in cross-domain (best HAN: 0.645) than in cross-topic (best HAN: 0.753), while cross-temporal is described as showing favorable stability and the annotator section does not present a directly comparable difficulty setup.

Wikipedia crawl data. Specifically, they use Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” pages as seed sets, then iteratively crawl “See also,” “References,” and “External links” up to two hops, with negative seeds from Wikipedia’s random article endpoint.

Yes. They compare their semantic/neural approach against lexical baselines such as TfIdf-SVM and language models, and report that the neural methods compare favorably or outperform lexical models on several metrics.
About 64 million segments from YouTube videos, comprising 1.2 billion tokens, were used for training.
About 64 million segments from YouTube videos, comprising a total of 1.2 billion tokens.
1500-dimensional frame-level visual feature vectors were extracted from the video frames at 1-second intervals, similar to features used for large-scale image classification tasks.

Yes. The main NMT models use the “scaled Luong” attention mechanism, and the paper also mentions experimenting with a Transformer model, which was used in the Facebook Research integration experiments.
An established neural machine translation architecture based on LSTMs with the attention mechanism was explored. They also experimented with the Transformer model, but its results were worse.
Symbolic rewriting is the task of learning or performing formal-to-formal translation of symbolic data using rewrite rules. The paper motivates it as relevant to automated reasoning.

They incorporate expert knowledge by letting users specify domain-relevant anchor terms, such as disease-specific words like “diabetes,” “insulin,” or “metformin,” and adding a mutual-information term between these anchor variables and latent factors in the CorEx objective. This anchors or seeds topics, imposes semantics on latent factors, and guides the model to discover other words associated with the expert-specified concepts.
20 Newsgroups and the i2b2 2008 Obesity Challenge dataset.

No. The paper discusses related topic models such as SeededLDA in the related work section, but the reported experimental comparisons are against Unsupervised CorEx and Naive Bayes, not popular topic models like LDA.

Peng and Dredze (2016) is the state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media. The paper’s integrated training model (F-Score Driven Model II) achieves a new state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media.
Sina Weibo service in China.

They used a modified labeled Weibo NER corpus, as in Peng and Dredze (2016), along with the same unlabeled text from the Sina Weibo service in China. The labeled data include train, development, and test sets, and the unlabeled data contain 112,971,734 Weibo messages.
XNLI (Cross-lingual NLI), MLDoc (Document Classification), Cross-lingual Intent Classification, POS Tagging, and Named Entity Recognition.

Yes. The paper states that the gains are more pronounced in low resource languages, and concludes that the positive language transfer seen in improved translation quality for low resource languages is also reflected in cross-lingual transferability.

No. In the analysis, the paper contrasts “training one model for each language” with “one model for all languages” for POS tagging, indicating mBERT is not necessarily fine-tuned separately for each language.

They did not select a fixed set of 50 languages directly; the paper says the experiments cover “more than 50 languages” across 5 tasks. For POS tagging, they explicitly chose 48 languages by taking the intersection of languages available in Universal Dependencies v2.3 and the languages supported by their mNMT model.

They use both automatic and human evaluations. Automatic evaluation uses perplexity and BLEU against human-annotated responses, while human evaluation uses Likert-style scoring and ACUTE-EVAL self-chat pairwise comparisons on engagingness, interestingness, and humanness.
A significant gap. In human evaluation, the multilingual model achieved nearly a 100 percent winning rate against the cross-lingual XNLG model.
They compare against a translation-based Poly-encoder pipeline using Google Translate API: target-language query → English query → English response → target-language response.
Chinese, French, Indonesian, Italian, Korean, and Japanese.
Yes. They automatically created their own contrastive test set from the OpenSubtitles corpus by extracting examples and introducing contrastive translations.

The baselines are standard context-agnostic sentence-level models: for recurrent models, a bidirectional RNN with attention trained with Nematus; for Transformer models, a standard Transformer-base model. The paper also considers simple concatenation baselines, concat22 for both architectures and concat21 for Transformers.

The context-aware models experimented were RNN-based concat22, s-hier, s-t-hier, and s-hier-to-2, and Transformer-based concat22, concat21, and the Voita et al. (2018) source-context model. The paper also mentions experimenting with source-side and target-side additional context, and with weight tying for the RNN variants.
English→German.

They use a simple entity linking algorithm that directly links each mention to the entity with the greatest commonness score, where commonness is computed from Wikipedia anchor links as the probability of an entity given the mention string. They also use this commonness score as the confidence of the linking result, and within the same document they apply a heuristic to corefer generic person mentions to more specific ones.

They use a two-layer BiLSTM-based architecture for context encoding, combined with mention string and KB type representations, whose concatenation is fed into an MLP with three dense layers for prediction.
FIGER (GOLD) and BBN. They do not use OntoNotes.
The evaluation uses precision, recall, and F1-score for different labels, along with overall F1-score and accuracy. It also reports accuracy without the ‘O’-tag.
The fine-tuned BERT model achieves an F1-score of 0.89 on the concept recognition dataset, using an 80-20 train-evaluation split.
Unanswerable

The labeling scheme is a BIO labelling scheme with 11 concept types: abb, grp, syscon, opcon, seterm, event, org, art, cardinal, loc, and mea. These represent abbreviations, groups/individuals, system concepts, operational concepts, generic SE terms, events, organizations, artifacts/instruments, numerical values, locations, and measures/features/behaviors, respectively.
BERT’s pre-trained cased language model.
The constructed concept recognition dataset contains roughly 3700 annotated sentences.
Precision, recall and F-measure.
Teufel et al.’s hand-crafted features include the position of sentence, sentence length, and tense. Widyantoro et al. used content features, qualifying adjectives, and meta-discourse features.

The paper uses word2vec-based embeddings: averaged word vectors learned by the classical word2vec algorithm, paragraph vectors (PV-DM/ParaVec), and BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE). It also reports using Brown model embeddings.
No. They use existing datasets: the ACL Anthology Reference Corpus and MixedAbs for training, and the AZ corpus, which consists of 80 AZ-annotated conference articles, for testing.

Sentence embeddings are generated using three explored models: by averaging the word vectors in a sentence, by using paragraph vectors (PV-DM/ParaVec), and by averaging BAS-specific word embeddings. In the averaging models, word embeddings are learned with word2vec (or BSWE for BAS-specific embeddings) and then averaged over the sentence.

Argumentative zoning is rhetorical sentence classification: the process of assigning rhetorical status to extracted sentences. In this paper, the rhetorical statuses are seven categories assigned to full sentences: aim, contrast, own, background, other, basis, and textual.

They crawled two credit-based freemium blackmarket sites, YouLikeHits and Like4Like, from February to April 2019, created dummy accounts with IRB approval, and recorded the Tweet IDs of tweets posted there for retweets. They then used Twitter’s REST API to collect the tweet objects, and also collected the authors’ timelines to find genuine tweets by the same users.

They compare against state-of-the-art Twitter spam detection methods and standard classifiers trained on their generated features. Specifically, the baselines are Wu et al. [5] (“Spam Detection 1”), Rajdev et al. [12] with Random Forest (“Spam Detection 2”), and feature concatenation fed to Random Forest, MLP, and SVM.
English
They look at freemium blackmarket services, specifically three categories: social-share services, credit-based services, and auto-time retweet services.

They use a two-language setting with English and Chinese, and a three-language setting with English, French, and Chinese during pretraining. In the first pretraining stage, they initialize from a 15-language pre-trained XLM.
The decoder is a 6-layer Transformer decoder with 1024 hidden units, 8 attention heads, and GELU activations in each layer.
The encoder is a 10-layer Transformer encoder. For each Transformer layer, it uses 1024 hidden units, 8 attention heads, and GELU activations.

The baselines are XLM and pipeline methods built on XLM; for supervised English-English question generation they also compare against CorefNQG and MP-GSN. In zero-shot settings, the pipeline baseline translates inputs and/or outputs with either their Transformer translator or Google Translator.

They look at human evaluation via Amazon Mechanical Turk using a triple pairing task, where judges select the correct pairing between shuffled prompts and stories. They also use a blind human preference comparison to evaluate the importance of hierarchical generation, comparing hierarchically generated stories against stories generated without a prompt.
Perplexity and prompt ranking accuracy.

Language models (non-hierarchical, not conditioned on the prompt) using the gated convolutional language model (GCNN) of Dauphin et al. (2017) and their added self-attention; seq2seq models using LSTMs, convolutional seq2seq, and Conv seq2seq with decoder self-attention; an ensemble of two Conv seq2seq with self-attention models; and a KNN baseline that retrieves the closest training prompt using TF-IDF vectors built with FASTTEXT and searched with FAISS.

No. The paper mentions training on top of a pre-trained seq2seq model via a fusion mechanism, and the appendix lists an embedding size of 256, but it does not mention using pre-trained embeddings like BERT.
The premise is generated using the convolutional language model from Dauphin et al. (2017).
Yes. The dataset was collected from Reddit’s Writing Prompts forum, where users submit story premises and other users respond with stories.
From Reddit’s Writing Prompts forum.
The experiments used five pre-trained embeddings: word2vec, fastText, GloVe, Baroni, and SL999.

SICK, STSB, and MRPC (Microsoft Research Paraphrase Corpus) were used. SICK was used for textual entailment recognition and also semantic textual similarity, STSB for semantic textual similarity, and MRPC for paraphrase identification.

DT TEAM (Maharjan et al., 2017), ECNU (Tian et al., 2017), BIT (Wu et al., 2017), TF-KLD (Ji and Eisenstein, 2013), Multi-Perspective CNN (He et al., 2015), InferSent (Conneau et al., 2017), GRAN (Wieting and Gimpel, 2017), Paragram-Phrase (Wieting et al., 2016b), and HCTI (Shao, 2017).
Fleiss’s Kappa.
Yes. They report inter-annotator agreement in Table 3 and state that they computed reliability metrics using Fleiss’s Kappa for the three annotators.

The social-network features are five neighborhood-overlap measures between author and target in the directed following network: downward, upward, inward, outward, and bidirectional overlap. They also use user-based features from author and target profiles: number of friends, number of followers, verified status, and number of tweets posted within six-month timeline snapshots.
aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance

Cyberbullying is defined in the document by the criteria of aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance. It is also discussed as an extension of traditional bullying, which is commonly defined by repetition, harmful intent, and an imbalance of power.

The output was evaluated for similarity to training data using Phrase Overlap match and K-gram-Jaccard similarity, for syntactic correctness using the Link Grammar Parser, and for humor/quality subjectively using human judgment. They also reported syntactic accuracy as the percentage of sentences having at least one valid linkage at Null Count 0.
The joke data came from two sources: CrowdTruth and Subreddits.
Inspirational quotes.
They use filters of size 1 × 3 in the convolution layer. The model uses multiple such 1 × 3 filters.

On FB15k-237, they report significant improvements over the previous state-of-the-art ConvKB of 0.105 MRR (about 25.1% relative improvement) and 6.1% absolute Hits@10. The abstract only states that CapsE obtains better performance than previous state-of-the-art models on WN18RR and FB15k-237.
No. In the experiments, they only used BERT and BERT-based variants such as BERT + Bi-LSTM, BERT + HighwayLSTM, BERT + DenseNet, BERT + BIMPM, and BERT + Sim-Transformer.
They compared BERT, BERT + Bi-LSTM, BERT + HighwayLSTM, BERT + DenseNet, an ensembled model, BERT + BIMPM, BERT + BIMPM (first bi-LSTM removed), and BERT + Sim-Transformer.
CoNLL03, Yahoo Answer Classification Dataset, and Quora-Question-Pair.

They report high inter-annotator agreement across two phases: raw agreement for scene/function/construal was .92/.95/.90 in Phase 1 and .93/.90/.89 in Phase 2. Cohen’s kappa was .90/.93/.88 in Phase 1 and .92/.88/.88 in Phase 2.

They annotated the Chinese translation of The Little Prince manually for SNACS adpositional supersenses: after Jieba tokenization, they manually corrected segmentation so potential adpositions were separate tokens, and all annotators jointly identified adposition targets. The corpus was jointly annotated by three native Mandarin Chinese speakers; 25% of targets were labeled cooperatively by all three, and the rest were labeled independently by all three and then adjudicated cooperatively, with annotations stored in CoNLL-U-Lex alongside StanfordNLP POS tags and dependency trees.
The final corpus includes all 27 chapters of The Little Prince, with a total of 20k tokens.

They use the training dataset containing five features: ID, title, author, text, and label, with about 20,800 samples. They do not use the provided test dataset because it has no labels, and instead randomly select a test set from the training data for evaluation.

The paper explores three pre-training text representation models: CountVectorizer, TF-IDFVectorizer, and Word2Vec, and five fine-tuning models: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), Long Short Term Memory networks (LSTMs), Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machine (SVM). In total, this yields 15 model combinations.

The essays are represented with six feature groups: numerical features, language-model probabilities, word clusters, topic-model features, part-of-speech tag features, and essay-text features. Specifically, these include counts and readability/error measures (e.g., sentences, words, letters, Gunning Fog index, difficult/misspelled/duplicate words, low-idf words), probabilities under two trigram language models, binary one-hot word-cluster vectors, concatenated LDA topic distributions, unigram POS bag-of-words, and bigram essay bag-of-words with idf weighting.
The evaluation metrics were the challenge’s official error metric E and accuracy. The paper reports stratified 3-fold cross-validation scores for both measures.
Gradient boosted trees trained with gradient-based one-side sampling, implemented in Microsoft’s Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM), are used.

Future work is to deeply investigate whether the usefulness of LDA topic-distribution features comes from LDA’s modeling power or from an artifact of the dataset, specifically possible information leakage from essay prompts tied to candidate level. The author suggests further analysis and validation, especially if essay topics are released so validation splits can be created based on topic.
For reference, the baseline was a tuned Logistic Regression with the same class weights for the class imbalance. Its performance with all features was Error (E) 10.10 and Accuracy 97.2.

Sentence alignment quality is evaluated by manual human evaluation of text-to-text alignments on a 5-point scale: 1 wrong alignment, 2 partial alignment with slightly compositional translational equivalence, 3 partial alignment with compositional translation and additional or missing information, 4 correct alignment with compositional translation and few additional or missing information, and 5 correct alignment and fully compositional translation. The corpus is split into three bins by hunalign confidence score, and two annotators each rate 30 items from each bin, with 10 overlapping items for inter-annotator reliability.

The speech alignment quality is evaluated by manual human evaluation of audio-text alignment on a 3-point scale: 1 = wrong alignment, 2 = partial alignment with some words or sentences possibly missing, and 3 = correct alignment, allowing non-spoken syllables at the start or end. Two annotators each rated 30 items from each of three bins, with 10 shared items for inter-annotator reliability.

Yes. The paper states that unlike selective attention, their selective gate is designed to handle bags with only one sentence by assigning a low gating value to wrongly labeled instances, preventing noisy representations from being propagated.
Unanswerable
Unanswerable
They evaluate on the SICK dataset from SemEval 2014 Task 1, for semantic relatedness and textual entailment.
Yes. In Section 5.4, they compare Tree LSTM, LSTM, and 1-Bidirectional LSTM both with and without the CNN layer, reporting standalone performance before CNN forwarding.
Unanswerable

SRE18 unlabeled data was used for the challenge as the asnorm dataset. The systems also used SRE, SWB, Voxceleb, SRE16, SRE18, MIXER6, Callhome, and Callfriend in training and backend components depending on the subsystem.

The fused subsystem outperformed the others. Among individual subsystems, eftdnn had the best overall performance, with the lowest EER and min-DCF on the SRE18 DEV set and the lowest EER on the SRE18 EVAL set.

Yes. They assume that when related words or phrases co-occur in the same text, their frequencies should be enhanced so the model can capture similarities between different expressions, effectively reducing language variation.
economic and political domains, and Islam.
WordNet and EuroVoc were used as thesauri in the approach. In the manual evaluation on the Islam portal, they used the RuThes thesaurus together with an additional Islam thesaurus.

They define hate speech as language used to express hatred toward a targeted group or intended to be derogatory, humiliating, or insulting to members of that group. In extreme cases, it may also threaten or incite violence, but they do not limit the definition only to such cases.
A logistic regression model with L2 regularization in a one-versus-rest framework.
33,458 Twitter users.
Their dataset consists of 85.4 million tweets collected from 33,458 Twitter users, from which they manually labeled a random sample of 24,802 tweets.
SST-2. For the large variants of BERT and RoBERTa, freezing 12–16 layers consistently increased quality, implying that fine-tuning all layers hurt performance.
Yes. They study both base and large variants of RoBERTa, and report that for the large models only 7 of 24 RoBERTa layers need fine-tuning to reach 90% of full-model quality.

On the Human Judged datasets, their method improves over the baseline by up to 0.61 points on SICK (53.45% vs. 52.07%, SNLI-trained) and up to 7.41 points on JOCI (48.59% vs. 41.18%, MultiNLI-trained). The gains specifically mentioned are that MultiNLI-trained models outperform the baseline on JOCI for all tested smooth values, SNLI-trained models exceed the baseline on JOCI with smooth 0.01 and 0.1, and with larger smooth terms both SNLI- and MultiNLI-trained models exceed the baseline on SICK.
Unanswerable

It mitigates the bias pattern by making artifacts balanced across labels through sample reweighting: each training example is assigned a weight based on the inverse of the estimated hypothesis-only label probability, using \(w = 1 / ((1-\epsilon)P(y|h)+\epsilon)\) with normalization to keep label priors unchanged. The framework estimates \(P(y|h)\) via K-fold cross-prediction and then trains the model with these weights so that dependence on annotation artifacts is reduced.
They use SNLI, MultiNLI, JOCI, and SICK for cross-dataset testing. Specifically, models are trained and validated on SNLI and MultiNLI, and tested on SICK and JOCI.

The entropy strategy was the most helpful, reaching optimal performance the quickest for both logistic regression and random forest. Query-by-committee was only slightly better than random selection for machine learning ensembles, and no tested strategy was useful for CNN or deep learning ensembles.
3,685,984 unique tweets.
10K sentences and 250K tokens.

The neural models explored were neural network-based POS taggers with embeddings plus either a feedforward network with a context window or a bidirectional LSTM for encoding, and either a softmax layer with greedy decoding or a CRF layer with Viterbi decoding for prediction. They also experimented with additional features: prefixes, suffixes, and character embeddings composed with a max-pooled CNN.

The rule-based model evaluated was the rule-based tagger by Rashel et al. It tags named entities and multi-word expressions using a dictionary, uses MorphInd to tag remaining words, and applies 15 hand-crafted rules for post-processing ambiguous tags.

PANL10N and the IDN Tagged Corpus have been used for Indonesian POS tagging. PANL10N consists of 15K manually tagged sentences, while the IDN Tagged Corpus contains 10K sentences and 250K manually tagged tokens.
They train the embeddings on parts of Chinese Wikipedia articles, resulting in 11,529,432 segmented words.
No. They use only two test tasks: word similarity and word analogy.
Unanswerable

The paper identifies that the model lacked flexibility for out-of-vocabulary words and that performance dropped substantially on an OOV test set. It also notes that unknown words were assigned a zero vector, which negatively affected testing results.
Yes. Section 3.4 reports evaluation results for “POS and morph classes” with precision, recall, and F1 scores, so morphology detection is evaluated as part of the POS tagging task.

It extends the standard ENAMEX type of organization, location, and person. The introduction also notes that named entity models can be made more complex by detecting numerical types such as percentages (NUMEX) or dates (TIMEX).
Gender, number, and case.
Yes. The reported corpus and evaluation are on Twitter users from a database of tweets by users who announced their pregnancy on Twitter, and no non-English datasets are mentioned.

The dataset is a sample of 10,417 Twitter users drawn from a database of over 400 million publicly available tweets posted by more than 100,000 users who had announced their pregnancy on Twitter. Two professional annotators labeled users as “bot,” “non-bot,” or “unavailable” based on their publicly available Twitter sites, with κ = 0.93 inter-annotator agreement on 1,000 overlapping annotations. After resolving disagreements, 413 (4%) users were labeled bot, 7,849 (75.35%) non-bot, and 2,069 (19.9%) unavailable.

By using a machine learning classifier on top of an existing bot detection system and adding simple features derived from health-related data. Specifically, the paper extends Botometer with features such as tweet diversity, URL score, mean daily posts, topics, mean post length, profile picture, and user name, which significantly improves bot detection performance for health-related Twitter users.

Adverse drug reaction detection, syndromic surveillance, subject recruitment for cancer trials, and characterizing drug abuse take place in social media. Earlier work focused mostly on population-level studies, while more recent work includes user-level longitudinal analysis such as mining health-related information from cohorts of pregnant women.
Unanswerable
The baseline model is the pre-trained version of BERT without special emoji tokens, specifically bert-base-multilingual-cased.
1,396,896 QA tuples.
They used a customer support dataset of 2000 question-answer tuples collected by Hori and Hori, sourced from Twitter. The abstract describes it as a corpus of QA tuples with a high number of emoji.

They used boosting via a Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM), described as fast gradient boosting on decision trees. The boosting combined predictions from the base classifiers with TF-IDF and QIEF features.
Yes. They compared general BERT embedding with domain-specific BioBERT embedding, and found BioBERT performed better.

The improved dataset, PICONET, contains 96,328 sequences in total. This is the sum of the category counts in Table 1: 22,818 (I) + 7 (I O) + 337 (I P) + 10,994 (O) + 30,106 (P) + 13 (P O) + 32,053 (NEGATIVE).

It was collected from PubMed structured abstracts by searching on April 11, 2019 for 363,078 English human clinical trial abstracts, then selecting abstract section headings that uniquely represented Population/Problem, Intervention, and Outcome. The authors lemmatized section labels, manually inspected samples to choose candidate labels, kept full abstract sections rather than splitting them into sentences, created multi-label instances for sections like “population and intervention,” added negative examples from headings such as “aim,” and cleaned the data with language identification, removal of missing values, unicode cleaning, and filtering sequences to 5–200 words.
Unanswerable

Previous datasets are described as having redundancy and ambiguity. In particular, the keyword-based approach in Jin and Szolovits (2018) could assign labels to sections that were not purely about a single PIO category, and it split multi-sentence labeled sections into individual sentences, which the paper argues can be poor candidates and less feature-rich.
Unanswerable

They explore all four agreement relation types: subject-verb agreement, noun-determiner agreement, noun-attributive adjective agreement, and subject-predicate adjective agreement. They examine these using the features number, gender, case, and person.
Amazon, Yelp, IMDB, MR, MPQA, Subj, and TREC.
word-LSTM, word-CNN, and a fine-tuned BERT base-uncased classifier; the baseline compared against is TextFooler.
It reduced error by 14% relative to prior work. The F1 also improved from 0.744 to 0.752.
Buschmeier, Cimiano, and Klinger (2014).
Baseline: All Sarcasm and Baseline: Random.
Twitter and Amazon product reviews.

Training data came from two domains: Twitter and Amazon product reviews. The Amazon reviews used the publicly available sarcasm corpus of Filatova (2012), and the Twitter data consisted of tweets collected in February–March 2016 containing exactly one trailing hashtag, with “#sarcasm” labeled sarcastic and “#happiness,” “#sadness,” “#anger,” “#surprise,” “#fear,” and “#disgust” labeled non-sarcastic; after filtering, both datasets were split 80% training and 20% testing.
LSTM and GRU with Attention was the best model, with a macro-F1 of 0.89 and weighted average F1 of 0.88. Its Offensive-class precision, recall, and F1 were 0.96, 0.68, and 0.77, respectively.

Two SVMs (linear and RBF), Stochastic Gradient Descent classifier, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, Bernoulli Naïve Bayes, Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT.

LSTM and GRU with Attention best performs on the offensive language identification task. It outperformed all other models in macro-F1 and in precision, recall, and F1 for both the Offensive and Not Offensive classes.
No. The annotations were done in LightTag by a team of three volunteers, with disagreements resolved by one author and two extra human judges.
3 annotators.
No. The OGTD v1.0 contains 1,401 offensive tweets and 3,378 not offensive tweets, with over 29% offensive content.

They experiment on five classical machine learning models: linear SVM, RBF SVM, Stochastic Gradient Descent classifier, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes. They also experiment on deep learning models: Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT.
No. The document describes two separate datasets of hotel reviews, one in Catalan and one in Basque, and does not mention any reviews containing translations for both languages.
910 reviews: 567 Catalan and 343 Basque.
2, with a third annotator resolving conflicts after the final iteration.

It works by first recognizing the sentence structure from dependency relations and POS tags, then mapping the important words to main components such as subject, verb, and object using ASP program Π2. Next, ASP program Π3 recursively finds complement components such as compound nouns, adjective modifiers, conjunctions, prepositional phrases, and adverbial modifiers to build maximal chunks supporting the main components. Finally, the GF Grammar Encoder assigns GF rules to the recognized structure and selected components, applying extended rules in a fixed order to construct the GF grammar and its supporting operators.

Generating natural language descriptions from annotated ontologies, such as Phylotastic workflows and a people ontology. Creating an intermediate GF-based representation for Wikipedia pages that can be translated back to English and used for cross-language translation.

Yes. They explore the structure of the learned language vectors across languages, showing that the vectors preserve similarity properties between languages and can reconstruct language family trees by clustering.
A standard stacked character-based LSTM with two layers, followed by a hidden layer and a final softmax output layer.

They show genetic relationships by hierarchically clustering the learned language vectors; for example, Figure 5 presents a clustering of Germanic languages that closely matches established genetic relationships in that family. The paper states that the language vectors capture similarity properties between languages and can reveal these relations without presupposing prior knowledge about language evolution.

No. They report only preliminary qualitative results from daily pharmacovigilance activities that MagiCoder “drastically reduces the amount of work required for the revision of a report,” while their formal experiment compared automatic encoding against previously manually validated reports rather than testing time reduction on real pharmacologists.

Yes. They suggest that longer descriptions do not usually have a complex structure, but because MagiCoder does not take into account sentence structure or logical connectives, performance drops on very long descriptions while it behaves very well on very short and short ones.

The MagiCoder algorithm has five phases: definition of ad hoc data structures; preprocessing of the original text with tokenization, stemming, and stop-word removal; a word-by-word linear scan with a voting task in which words vote for LLTs they belong to; weight calculation for recognized terms based on syntactic matching information; and sorting/pruning of voted terms followed by release of the winning terms. In the structured description, this includes creating meta-dictionaries, preprocessing the narrative description, scanning words and recording votes/positions, computing four weights, and then selecting, ordering, and releasing winning terms.

The system builds hash-table meta-dictionaries from the terminology, mapping each word (and stem) to the set of LLTs containing it, so dictionary lookup is constant time after a one-time O(km) construction. Then it scans the narrative description word by word only once, voting for matching terms and tracking positions, so the processing is linear in the input text, while avoiding approximate matching to preserve direct-access efficiency.

The authors conclude that computational linguistic analysis of first-person social media accounts can help draw a more complete picture of the aspects and mechanisms of personal recovery in bipolar disorder. They also expect that studying recovery views from previously under-researched ethnic, language, and cultural groups will yield valuable insights into how appropriate the recovery approach is for a wider population.
Unanswerable

The analysis used comparative corpus analysis with Wmatrix for keywords and key concepts, an iterative dictionary-based approach with semantic tagging using USAS, and distributional semantics to find co-occurring terms. It also mentions unsupervised topic modelling via DLATK and emotion/sentiment analysis using supervised machine learning, including multilingual embedding-based transfer.
No. The paper states that they will not ask the Twitter and Reddit account holders included in the datasets for their consent.

Twitter and Reddit users with a bipolar disorder diagnosis will be identified automatically via self-reported diagnosis statements, such as “I was diagnosed with BD-I last week.” Relevant blogs will be manually identified, and their authors will be contacted to obtain informed consent for using their texts.
The training/testing data comes from CCPC1.0. The paper states that the vocabulary is built from CCPC1.0 and analyzes the distribution of CI forms over CCPC1.0.

The generated Chinese poetry types are the two primary categories: SHI and CI. For SHI, the major forms are Jueju and Lvshi, including Wuyan Jueju, Qiyan Jueju, Wuyan Lvshi, and Qiyan Lvshi; for CI, the system generates 121 major forms (Cipai).

They compare mainly to subword-based NMT using BPE from Sennrich, Haddow, and Birch (2015b) and to a fully character-based baseline implementing Lee, Cho, and Hofmann (2016). In related work they also discuss Luong and Manning (2016)’s hybrid character-word model and prior morphology-aware MT work, but the explicit experimental baselines are BPE and the character-based system.
Yes. They train both skip-gram (SG) and CBOW using the word2vec tool.

Quality is measured by evaluation tasks on the learned word vectors: word similarity, synonym selection, and word analogy. In the word similarity task, quality is measured by the Pearson correlation coefficient between human similarity judgments and cosine distances in vector space, where a higher coefficient indicates better vectors.
No. They report results on multiple datasets, including news datasets KPTimes and JPTimes as well as KPCrowd, DUC-2001, SemEval, and KP20k.

They were collected by crawling selected online news websites, primarily The New York Times. The authors retrieved URLs of free-to-read New York Times articles from 2006 to 2017 and collected the corresponding archived HTML pages using the Internet Archive; a secondary source was the Japan Times.
FirstPhrase. It extracts the first N keyphrase candidates from a document.

Two generative neural keyphrase generation models are trained: CopySci and CopyNews. They use the same CopyRNN encoder-decoder architecture with a copying mechanism, with CopySci trained on scientific abstracts and CopyNews trained on newspaper articles.

The editors’ annotations are more uniform and consistent than those in existing datasets, due in part to the use of tag suggestions, whereas author and reader annotations use a larger, less controlled vocabulary. Editors also tend to assign more generalized keyphrases that often do not appear in the document, with frequent tags resembling topic descriptors.
Unanswerable
Yes. They initialize the word embeddings with pre-trained GloVe vectors.
Unanswerable
F-measure. They report results as F-measure percentages on SensEval-3.
They used four datasets: DUC-2001, Inspec, the NUS Keyphrase Corpus, and the ICSI Meeting Corpus.
Their method improves over other state-of-the-art models by more than 2% in F1-score.
SingleRank and Topical PageRank.

They do not directly verify that the word alignments are correct. The paper assumes the well-trained autoregressive teacher model provides good attention-based word alignment information, and trains the non-autoregressive student to match the teacher’s encoder-decoder attention distributions via KL-divergence.
The paper reports the autoregressive Transformer baseline has a latency of 784 ms. The proposed NART model with hints is 26 ms, giving a 30.2× speedup over the ART counterpart.
BLEU is used to measure translation accuracy.
Yes. Besides WMT14, the model was also evaluated on IWSLT14 German-to-English (De-En).
Yes. The experiments are conducted on multiple German-English directions: IWSLT14 German-to-English, WMT14 English-to-German, and reversed WMT14 German-to-English.

The most discriminating features are average, maximum, minimum, and standard deviation, with the highest information gain obtained by average, maximum, and minimum, and standard deviation. Probability and proportionality features have low information gain.

On the alternate dataset, the DSLCC development set, the proposed LDR obtains results comparable to Skip-gram and SenVec, supporting its robustness. LDR accuracies are: Bulgarian 99.9, Macedonian 99.9, Spain Spanish 84.7, Argentina Spanish 88.0, Portugal Portuguese 87.4, Brazilian Portuguese 90.0, Bosnian 78.0, Croatian 85.8, Serbian 86.4, Indonesian 99.4, Malay 99.2, Czech 99.8, Slovak 99.3, and Other languages 99.9.

No. During fine-tuning, they extract answer spans by looking for occurrences of the gold standard answer in the provided snippets, but this is “not perfect” and can produce false negatives when “a sentence answers the question, but the exact string used is not in the synonym list”; no coverage percentage is reported.
Unanswerable

SGNN (as proposed by Li et al. (2018a)) is used for the script event prediction task, with the input event embeddings replaced by the intent and sentiment-enhanced event embeddings. For stock market prediction, the section only compares against event-driven baseline methods and does not specify the prediction method used.

It is evaluated on the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset, where given an existing event context, the system chooses the most reasonable subsequent event from a candidate list. Following Li et al. (2018a), the paper uses the SGNN framework and replaces its input event embeddings with their intent- and sentiment-enhanced event embeddings.

The paper uses the hard similarity dataset from Weber et al. (2018) with 230 event pairs, an extended hard similarity dataset of 1,000 event pairs annotated by the authors, the transitive sentence similarity dataset from Kartsaklis and Sadrzadeh (2014) with 108 pairs of transitive sentences, and the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset for script event prediction. For stock market prediction, the specific dataset is not named in the provided document.
No. They compare against PPMI-SVD, SGNS, and their own character-level variants (C2V-NO-ATT and CHAR2VEC), and there is no mention of fastText or other subword-information models.

Yes. The paper says results are only reported for English, which is morphologically impoverished, and that this is unfortunate because the model performs better on words with richer morphology; it therefore expects even better performance for morphologically richer languages such as Turkish and German.
English. They expect it to perform even better for morphologically richer languages such as Turkish and German.

The embeddings are evaluated by ranking word pairs according to the cosine similarity of their word vectors and then measuring the correlation with human judgments using Spearman’s ρ. The comparison uses human similarity judgment datasets where annotators rate how similar two words are on a fixed scale.
15.6/16.5 (accuracy/NDCG) on MCTest-150 and 7.3/4.6 on MCTest-500.

They compared to Addition, Addition-proj, Neural Reasoner (NR), and Attentive Reader (AR). Addition and Addition-proj are simple distributed-representation baselines, while NR and AR are prior top-performing deep neural QA models.

The baseline is a one-stage RNN system with the same RNN structure as the last stage, containing a 2-layer BLSTM, and it is directly trained to recognize dialect category. In Table 2, this baseline is reported as “Baseline BLSTM.”

They report that both multi-stage systems outperform the baseline by about 10% relative ACC on every task, with the two-stage system best: baseline 78.85% overall (77.60% for ≤3s, 80.10% for >3s), two-stage 88.88% overall (87.72%, 90.04%), and three-stage 87.24% overall (85.52%, 88.96%). The two-stage system is more robust on short utterances, while both multi-stage systems perform excellently on long-duration speech; dialect-specific accuracies include Shanghai 98.8% and Hefei 99.8%.
Ningxia, Hefei, Sichuan, Shanxi, Changsha, Hebei, Nanchang, Shanghai, Kekka, and Fujian.
An ensemble of four neural machine translation models with Transformer architecture was used.
Unanswerable
It entirely relies on neural models and does not use any external NLP tools, spell checkers, or hand-crafted confusion sets.
Building Educational Applications

There is no clear winner between the two proposed systems according to human evaluation. MODULAR is better at reducing bias and has higher accuracy, while CONCURRENT produces more fluent responses, preserves meaning better, and has higher BLEU.

They were a Wikipedia community effort: the corpus was harvested from aligned sentences pre- and post-neutralization by English Wikipedia editors. The paper does not say annotators were trained to perform those edits; trained crowdworkers were only used later for qualitative evaluation.

Subjective text is automatically neutralized by generating an output sentence that preserves the input’s meaning as much as possible while removing subjective bias. The paper proposes two encoder-decoder methods: MODULAR, which first uses a BERT-based classifier to detect subjective words and then an LSTM-based editor with a join embedding to revise them, and CONCURRENT, which uses a BERT encoder and directly generates the neutralized sentence.

Word-level American Sign Language recognition is investigated, using a multimodal dataset (GMU-ASL51) with 51 word-level classes. The work studies ASL recognition from skeletal data and RGB video data, including their combination.
The best model is Spatial AI-LSTM with 81% average cross-subject accuracy.
